questions.optionB: Severe transfusion-dependent anemia by 6 months of age, hepatosplenomegaly, chipmunk facies (extramedullary hematopoiesis causing frontal bossing and maxillary hypertrophy)
questions.explanation: β-thalassemia major: absent β-globin chains (β0/β0) → severe anemia presenting at 6 months (when HbF declines). Features: severe microcytic hypochromic anemia (Hb 3-7 g/dL), hepatosplenomegaly (extramedullary hematopoiesis), chipmunk/thalassemic facies (frontal bossing, maxillary prominence, depressed nasal bridge — marrow expansion), hair-on-end/crew-cut skull XR. HbF elevated (>90%), HbA absent. Treatment: regular transfusions (monthly, target Hb 9-10.5), iron chelation (deferasirox/deferoxamine — prevents iron overload), folic acid. Cure: HSCT (bone marrow transplant). Reference: OP Ghai — Thalassemia.

questions.questionText: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) is the most common childhood malignancy and presents with:
questions.optionB: Pallor, bleeding (petechiae, mucosal bleeding), bone/joint pain, fever, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy — due to marrow infiltration by blasts
questions.explanation: ALL: most common childhood cancer (25% of all childhood malignancies, peak 2-5 years). Presentation: marrow failure — anemia (pallor, fatigue), thrombocytopenia (bleeding, petechiae, bruising), neutropenia (fever, infections). Organ infiltration: hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, bone/joint pain (limping child), CNS involvement (headache, cranial nerve palsies), testicular involvement. Diagnosis: peripheral smear (blasts), bone marrow biopsy (>20-25% blasts), flow cytometry (B-cell ALL: CD10/CD19/CD20+). Treatment: induction (vincristine, prednisone, L-asparaginase, daunorubicin), CNS prophylaxis, maintenance. Prognosis: >90% long-term survival in children. Reference: OP Ghai — ALL.
